gpt_review.biasedText: They are not, contrary to widespread belief, universally eager to turn over every last shred of their intellectual and community-building capacity to robot servants/overlords.
gpt_review.reason: The line generalizes student attitudes and frames a contested claim as a corrective fact, using loaded phrasing (“robot servants/overlords”). It’s a defensible bias finding, though this is an opinion essay.

gpt_review.biasedText: This all runs counter to the doomy picture of helplessness painted last spring and summer by well-circulated stories about generative A.I.'s effect on higher education.
gpt_review.reason: “Doomy picture of helplessness” is emotionally charged framing of media coverage. The rhetoric heightens tone beyond neutral description.
This phrasing appears within a source’s quoted remark (Stuart Selber), not the author’s narrative voice. Per rubric, quoted source language should not be treated as author bias.

gpt_review.biasedText: a horseshoe-crab-like relic of primordial ed tech that A.I. has saved from extinction
gpt_review.reason: Colorful, value-laden metaphor that characterizes blue books as obsolete. This is subjective spin via vivid diction.

gpt_review.biasedText: All of the bigger forces in our culture are pulling people away from deeply attentive reading
gpt_review.reason: This statement is attributed to Scott Saul in quotation. As quoted source speech, it should not be counted as the author’s biased prose per the instructions.

gpt_review.biasedText: Such tech-assisted breezing through a text is to reading a novel roughly as listening to a podcast about marriage is to being married.
gpt_review.reason: A dramatic analogy that strongly disparages “tech-assisted” reading. It’s hyperbolic and emotionally loaded.

gpt_review.biasedText: the ultraprocessed flavor of A.I. in a paper
gpt_review.reason: Metaphoric phrasing with negative connotations frames AI writing as artificial and inferior. Clear subjective spin.

gpt_review.biasedText: students ... are also more anxious and isolated
gpt_review.reason: Emphasizes negative traits of current students without parallel positives in that sentence; a generalization that tilts tone negatively.

gpt_review.biasedText: break the funereal hush of isolatoes bent in solitude over their phones
gpt_review.reason: Pejorative descriptors (“funereal hush,” “isolatoes”) heighten negativity toward device use via charged word choice.

gpt_review.biasedText: The cellphone and now A.I. have made college a lonelier experience than it used to be
gpt_review.reason: Causal, sweeping claim about technology’s social effects stated declaratively without evidence; a subjective judgment framed as fact.

gpt_review.biasedText: Students feel pressure to use college to prepare intensively for a particular career, like training full-time as an amateur to play a sport professionally, but they will graduate into a working world where the games and rules keep changing.
gpt_review.reason: Broad generalization about motivations and labor-market unpredictability treated as given; defensibly flagged as opinion-as-fact.

gpt_review.biasedText: after first going fetal for far too long when assailed by return-on-investment literalists who willfully misunderstand the fit between school and work
gpt_review.reason: Loaded, derisive framing (“going fetal,” “ROI literalists,” “willfully misunderstand”) imputes motive and disparages critics. Clear spin.

gpt_review.biasedText: This tech-obsessed moment may have finally pushed humanists to identify the value of their disciplines and play to their strengths, after first going fetal for far too long when assailed by return-on-investment literalists...
gpt_review.reason: Presents one-sided framing sympathetic to humanists and hostile to critics, with no balancing perspective. Reasonable to flag as slant in an opinion piece.
